FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

This letter SUPERSEDES Carvalho Advice Letters Nos., A-02-293; A-02-294; and A-02-314

April 6, 2010

M. Lois Bobak
Woodruff, Spradlin & Smart
555 Anton Boulevard, Suite 1200
Costa Mesa, California 92626-7670

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-10-036

Dear Ms. Bobak:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Yorba Linda City
Councilmember Jan Horton regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act of the "Act")¹ and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the
"Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1
FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not
address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict
of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTIONS

1. May Councilmember Horton participate in the city council and/or redevelopment
agency's consideration of the zoning and/or possible development of the Lakeview/Altrudy
Property for residential uses, a fire station, and/or commercial uses?

2. May Councilmember Horton participate in the city council and/or redevelopment
agency's consideration of the zoning and/or possible development of the Stater Bros site for the
same or other uses?

CONCLUSIONS

1. & 2. No. Because Councilmember Horton's real property is located within 500 feet of
each of the properties that is the subject of the governmental decision, she is presumed to have a

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-10-036
Page No. 2

conflict of interest in participating in the governmental decisions. You have not provided
sufficient facts to rebut that presumption.

FACTS

The Yorba Linda City Council serves as the Board of Directors of the Yorba Linda
Redevelopment Agency. Councilmember Horton and her family own a home located at 4791
Cedar Avenue in the City of Yorba Linda (the "City"). The City has a redevelopment project
area that encompasses most of the City's downtown area. Councilmember Horton's family
home is located outside of, but immediately adjacent to, one corner of the northwest portion of
the redevelopment project area. The City also has a Town Center Master Plan. The area covered
by that plan overlaps much of the redevelopment project area. The City is currently developing a
Specific Plan for the Town Center. That plan will contain the zoning and development standards
for the properties covered by the plan.

Portions of two parcels of property located within the area covered by the Specific Plan
are within 500 feet of Councilmember Horton's home. The first is an undeveloped parcel
commonly referred to as the "Lakeview/Altrudy Property." The property is being considered for
possible development with residential uses, a new fire station, commercial uses, or some
combination thereof. The second property is a portion of a larger lot presently improved with a
"Stater Bros." grocery store. Possible redevelopment of the Stater Bros. building could range
from a relatively simple remodel of the building to a complete teardown and reconstruction.
Only commercial uses are being considered for this site.

Within the next several months, the city council/redevelopment board will consider
possible development/redevelopment if the Lakeview/Altrudy Property and/or the Stater Bros.
site. These discussions may take place in the context of broader discussions regarding the Town
Center Specific Plan. The discussions could range from consideration of preferred uses once
zoning designations are selected.

Councilmember Horton has retained a professional appraiser to undertake an analysis of
the possible impacts of development or redevelopment of the Lakeview/Altrudy Property and/or
the Stater Bros. site on her home. The written appraisals conclude that the possible
development/redevelopment will not have any positive or negative impact. You have provided a
copy of both the appraisal relating to the Lakeview/Altrudy Property as well as the appraisal
relating to the Stater Bros. site.

POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.

File No. A-10-036
Page No. 3

The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a governmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.

STEPS 1 & 2: IS COUNCILMEMBER HORTON A PUBLIC OFFICIAL MAKING, PARTICIPATING IN
MAKING, OR INFLUENCING A GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?

As a city councilmember/redevelopment agency director, Councilmember Horton is a
public official under the Act. (Section 82048.) Consequently, she may not make, participate in
making, or otherwise use her official position to influence any decisions that will have
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of her economic interests. Because
Councilmember Horton will be called upon to consider proposed projects before the city council,
she will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using her official position to influence a
governmental decision.²

STEP 3: DOES COUNCILMEMBER HORTON HAVE A POTENTIALLY DISQUALIFYING ECONOMIC
INTEREST?

A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section
87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests, including:

• An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
• An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
• An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.)
• An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)

² If a public official's office is listed in section 87200 ("87200 filers" include planning commission
members) and he or she has a conflict of interest in a decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: (1)
immediately prior to the discussion of the item, verbally identify each type of economic interest involved in the
decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in regulation 1802.5(b)(1)(B), on the record of the
meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the
item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal
interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5, subdivisions (c) and (d) apply. (Section 87105.)

File No. A-10-036
Page No. 4

• An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family — this is the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18703.5.)

You have indicated that Councilmember Horton has a real property economic interest in
her home. You have not provided any information with respect to any other economic interest.
Accordingly, our analysis is limited to Councilmember Horton's economic interest in her home.

STEP 4: IS THE ECONOMIC INTEREST DIRECTLY OR INDIRECTLY INVOLVED IN THE
GOVERNMENTAL DECISION?³

"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial
effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the official's
economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involved in the governmental decision."
(Regulation 18704(a).) For governmental decisions that affect real property interests, the
standards set forth in regulation 18704.2 apply. (Regulation 18704(a)(2).) For governmental
decisions that affect business entities, the standards set forth in regulation 18704.1 apply.
(Regulation 18704(a)(1).)

Real Property:

Regulation 18704.2(a) states, in pertinent part:

"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:

"(1) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real
property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries)
of the property [that] is the subject of the governmental decision."

You have indicated that the lot on which Councilmember Horton's home is located is
fewer than 500 feet from each of the properties that will be the subject of the governmental
decisions. Therefore, because the areas are located within 500 feet of her home, the real property
is directly involved in the governmental decisions.

STEP 5: MATERIALITY STANDARD

A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) For directly involved real property, Regulation 18705.2(a)(1) provides that the
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property is presumed to be material.

_______________
³ If a public official's economic interest in not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered
"indirectly involved." (Regulation 18704(a).)

File No. A-10-036
Page No. 5

"The presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real
property." (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1). (Emphasis added.)

Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a one
penny effect. This is known as the "one-penny rule." In order to rebut the presumption, the
official must establish that the decision would not affect his or her property's value by even one
cent.

You have submitted two appraisal reports that purport to establish that the presumption
will be rebutted. The reports conclude, respectively, that the "possible development of the
'Lakeview/Altrudy Property'" and the "possible redevelopment/remodeling of 'the Stater
Brothers Shopping Center' will have no effect, negative or positive, on [Councilmember
Horton's real property]." (Emphasis added.)

The appraisal reports are each, in substantive part, prepared on a standard URAR⁴ form
commonly used for home sales financing, including the grid portion that forms the basis for the
sales comparison approach to value, which selects three "comparable sales" in order to measure
the market value of the subject property. Each report even states that the "intended use of this
appraisal report is for the lender/borrower to evaluate the property that is the subject of this appraisal
for a mortgage finance transaction." (Page 4 of URAR form under "Intended Use.") The
appraisal also states that it included a complete visual inspection of the exterior and interior of
the Horton property and apparently includes photographs⁵ of the Horton property, including the
pool, as well as the comparables used. The property is valued at $810,000. No explanation is
given to explain why the appraiser needed to perform an interior inspection of the Horton
property or provide information on sales of properties located more than 500 feet from the
boundaries of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision or why any of the
information contained in the URAR is relevant for any purpose other than to establish the market
value of the Horton property, which is not at issue here.

A "Supplemental Addendum" is attached to each URAR, which apparently attempts to
address the issue herein. Here the appraiser states that the purpose of the appraisal is "[t]o
determine if the development of the Lakeview/Altrudy property into residential properties, a fire
station, commercial properties or a combination of the three [or "the redevelopment/remodeling . . .
of the site known as the Stater Brothers Shopping Center"] will have any effect, positive or
negative, on [Councilmember Horton's real property]."⁶

_______________________

⁴ Uniform Residential Appraisal Report

⁵ The copies of what appear to be photographs that were provided to us are indistinguishable.

⁶ The appraisal reports are essentially identical except that one refers to the development of the
Lakeview/Altrudy Property and the other to the redevelopment of the Stater Brother Shopping Center. For purposes of
simplification in our further analysis, we will refer to the two different properties collectively as the
development/redevelopment of the properties that are the subject of the governmental decisions.

File No. A-10-036
Page No. 6

To make this determination, the appraiser then appears to list three "factors determining
the impact of the development/redevelopment [of the properties that are the subject of the
governmental decisions on Councilmember Horton's real property]." Presumably, these are the
only factors looked at by the appraiser to make his determination¹ that the
development/redevelopment of the properties that are the subject of the governmental decision
will have "no effect, negative or positive, on Councilmember Horton's real property." The
factors examined are: will the governmental decisions relating to the
development/redevelopment of the properties (1) affect the development, (2) affect the use, or
(3) result in a change to the character of the neighborhood of Councilmember Horton's real
property.

These same factors appear in Regulation 18705.2(b)(1), which provides the materiality
standard for indirectly involved real property interest and certain factors that may be used to
rebut that presumption. The regulation provides:

"(b) Indirectly involved real property interests.

"(1) Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is
presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there
are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect,
and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic
interest, such that it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered include, but are not
limited to, circumstances where the decision affects:

"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;

"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;

"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood." (emphasis added.)

What are listed in the regulation are examples of circumstances that are affected by a
governmental decision that could be used to rebut a presumption of no materiality for indirectly
involved property. While they may be appropriately considered to rebut a presumption of
materiality, they are certainly not the only factors to be considered. Here, however, the appraiser
treats these factors as if they are the exclusive determining factors to rebut a presumption of
materiality of the financial effect on directly involved property.

_________________________
⁷ At least as far as what is contained in the reports; no other analysis being provided.

File No. A-10-036
Page No. 7

Because the report is limited to these factors, it does not adequately address the issue of
whether the development/redevelopment of the properties will have any financial effect on
Councilmember Horton's real property (Regulation 18705.2(a)(1)). The report reaches the
conclusion that the development/redevelopment of the properties that are the subject of the
governmental decisions will have "no effect, negative or positive," on Councilmember Horton's
real property because the development/redevelopment will not cause an increase in the traffic in
the neighborhood, or increase the noise, or affect the view. While these are factors to consider in
any analysis, they are most certainly not the only factors to consider that may impact a property's
value, especially when the impact need only be one penny.

The appraisal provides no analysis or discussion of why the development/redevelopment
of the properties that are the subject of the governmental decision would not have even a one-
penny affect on the Horton property. The appraisal, in fact, does not even address the one-penny
materiality rule at all. That factor alone renders the appraisal inadequate for purposes of
rebutting the presumption of materiality, because it does not acknowledge the appropriate test to
be applied for directly involved property.

The analysis also asserts that the improvements will not be "similar enough to use as
comparable properties in determining property value" (Emphasis added). Apparently, the
appraiser raises this statement in support of his finding that the development/redevelopment will
have no financial effect on the Horton property. However, just because a property's value is
estimated by looking at sales of similar properties, it does not follow that development of
dissimilar (noncomparable) properties will have no impact on a specific property's value solely
because the improvements made do not consist of comparable single family homes. If so, that
would mean that a home's value would be impacted if a lesser quality house was built on the lot
behind it, but it would not if a fast-food restaurant was put in instead.

The sales comparison approach to value is premised on the fact that adjustments are made
for differences between similar (even identical or "model match") houses. One of those
adjustments is for location. The desirability of a property's location is, in part, determined by
what surrounds it. The closer a property to desirable surroundings — the higher its value; the
closer to undesirable surroundings — the lower its value. Yet here the appraisal does not identify
what the surroundings are going to be for the Lakeview/Altrudy property (other than to cite a
wide range of possibilities). If one does not know what is going to be built, how can one
possibly measure its financial impact on property nearby?

According to your facts, the Lakeview/Altrudy property "is being considered for possible
development with residential uses, a new fire station, commercial uses, or some combination
thereof." This leaves open a wide range of possibilities. Among residential uses it could include
anything from low income apartments, to condominium or townhouse type units, duplexes, or
single family residences of varying degrees of quality of construction. With respect to
commercial uses, it could theoretically include: an auto repair shop, a drug rehabilitation center,
professional office buildings, a tattoo parlor, restaurants (fast food or gourmet or anywhere in
between), high-end boutiques, discount stores, or any number of other businesses. Yet here, we
are being asked to accept that the vast differences among these various choices will have no

File No. A-10-036
Page No. 8

bearing, one way or the other, on the properties nearby because none of them can be seen, heard,
or felt (through increased traffic) and none can be used as comparable sales for measuring the
market value of the residential properties nearby.

There are other factors that can cause a property to increase or decrease in market value
besides those considered in the appraisal reports (or listed as "examples of" in Regulation
18705.2(b)(1)). There are even more when we are not measuring market value per se, but, rather,
a materiality threshold of only a penny. A governmental decision on property located within 500
feet of an official's property that would not cause a financial effect of a least one penny would
have to be completely inconsequential to the property involved. Perhaps the installation of a fire
hydrant two blocks over or a pathway to a park on the next street, when the official's property is
served by a different park already, or burdened by the pathway and is served by a different park altogether, would
qualify as situations where there is not even a "one-penny effect." Development
projects are not these types of decisions, and decisions involving the components of a development
or redevelopment project are generally of such a magnitude that they will rarely, if ever, not have a
least a one-penny financial effect on the residential properties nearby (within 500 feet).⁸

Even the smaller decision relating to the Stater Bros. raises the same questions. Here the
facts provide that "redevelopment of the Stater Bros. building could range from a relatively
simple remodel of the building to a complete teardown and reconstruction." It seems apparent
that most people buying homes in an $800,000 neighborhood would be willing to pay a penny
more for their purchase to have a nicer grocery store at which to shop. Yet the appraisal
essentially concludes that they would not, a conclusion that we cannot accept without some
support.

Finally, in determining that the market value of the Horton's property is $810,000, the
appraiser made site adjustments of $5 per square foot on the comparable properties. The most
comparable of the three properties listed is located about a half a mile away, and on the other
side of a major thoroughfare (Yorba Linda Boulevard). Because the size of the lot is larger than
the Horton property, the sale price of that home was adjusted at the aforementioned rate.
Additionally, a $2,500 adjustment was made because the home has a bathtub and/or shower in
the third bathroom. According to the appraisal reports, this means that a single square foot of lot
size has a financial effect more than 500 times ($50/.01), and a bathtub more that 250,000 times
($2,500/.01), on the value of the Horton property than the financial effect resulting from what
may be built within a few hundred feet from it. Again, this is difficult for us to accept.

For the foregoing reasons, the information provided by these appraisal reports is not
sufficient to rebut the presumption of materiality under Regulation 18705.2(a) and cannot be
_______________
⁸ To the extent that our previous advice has provided otherwise, those letters are rescinded, particularly the
Carvalho Advice Letter, Nos. A-02-293; A-02-294; and A-02-314 where we accepted an "appraisal" from a real
estate broker, not a licensed real estate appraiser and, therefore, not subject to the Uniform Standards of
Professional Appraisal Practice that form the basis for the law in California governing real estate appraisals, in
support of a finding that a new development project consisting of "approximately 24 residential neighborhoods, a
commercial center, and over 240 acres of parks and open spaces" located within 500 feet of several official's
properties (at least one of which appears to abut the project) would not have a one-penny financial effect on the
official's property.

File No. A-10-036
Page No. 9

relied upon by Councilmember Horton as proof "that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decisions will have any financial effect" on Councilmember Horton's property.

STEP 6: REASONABLY FORESEEABLE

Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more
than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, it is up to the public official to make the determination through a good faith
effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-113; Moock Advice Letter, No. A-01-140; O'Harra
Advice Letter, No. A-00-174.) However, given the magnitude of the governmental decisions
involved, coupled with the extremely low materiality threshold present, it is reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect of the decisions will meet the one-penny materiality
threshold.

STEPS SEVEN AND EIGHT: PUBLIC GENERALLY AND LEGALLY REQUIRED
PARTICIPATION

You have not given any facts indicating that either the "public generally" exception or
that the "legally required participation" exception would apply.

If you have any questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: William J. Lenkeit
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

"In order to determine if a governmental decision's reasonably foreseeable financial

(Regulation 1870a(a).) For governmental decisions that affect real property interests, the

Real Property:
Regulation 187 04.2(a) states, in pertinent part:
"(a) Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved in a governmental decision if any of the following apply:
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any part of that real
property, is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries)
of the property lthatl is the subject of the governmental decision."
You have indicated that the lot on which Councilmember Horton's home is located is
fewer than 500 feet from each of the properties that will be the subject of the governmental
decisions. Therefore, because the areas are located within 500 feet of her home, the real property
is directly involved in the governmental decisions.
Srnp5: MItrnHr,ITY STANDARD
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material. (Regulation
18700(a).) For directly involved real property, Regulation 18705.2(aXl) provides that the
financial effect of a governmental decision on real property is presumed to be material.
I If a public otficial's ectrnomic interest in not directly involved in a governmental decision, it is considered
"indirectly involved." (Regulation I 8704ta).)
"The presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real
property." (Regulation 18705.2(a)(l). (Emphasis added.)
Under this rule, the financial effect of the decision is material even if it has only a one
penny effect. This is known as the "one-penny ruIe." In order to rebut the presumption, the
official must establish that the decision would not affect his or her property;s ,alue by even one
cent.
You have submitted two appraisal reports that purport to establish that the presumption
will be rebutted. The reports conclude, respectively, that ihe "possible development of ttri
'Lakeview/Altrudy Property"' and the "possible redevelopment/remodeling oi ,the Stater
Brothers Shopping Center' will have no effect, negative or positive, on [Coluncilmember
Horton's real propertyl." (Emphasis added.)
The appraisal reports are each, in substantive part, prepared on a standard URARa form
commonly used for home sales financing, including the grid portion that forms the basis for the
sales comparison approach to value, which selects ihr"e icorrparable sales" in order to measure
the market value of the subject property. Each report even states that the "intended use of this
appraisal report is for the lender/client to evalu-ate the property that is the subject of this appraisal
for a mortgage finance transaction." (Page 4 of URARform under..Intendedbse.,,) The
appraisal also states that it included a complete visual inspection of the exterior and interior of
the Horton property and apparently includes photographss of the Horton property, including the
pool, as well as the comparables used. The property ii valued at $810,00b. 'M explanatioi is
given to explain why the appraiser needed to perform an interior inspection of the Horton
property or provide information on sales of properties located -or" ih* 500 feet from the
bo-undaries of the property that is the subject of the governmental decision or why any of the
information contained in the URAR is relevant for any purpose other than to establish the market
value of the Horton property, which is not at issue heie.
A "Supplemental Addendum" is attached to each URAR, which apparently attempts to
address the issue herein. Here the appraiser states that the purpose of ttre appraisai is ,1t1t
determine if the development of the LakeviedAltrudy property into residential properties, a fire
station, commercial properties or a combination of the tio"L toi "the redevelopmenUremodeling ..
of the site known as the Stater Brothers Shopping Center"] will have any effect, positive or
negative, on [Councilmember Horton's real property]." 6
' Uniform Residential Appraisal Report
5 The copies of what appear to be photographs that were provided to us are indistinguishable.
6 The appraisal reports are essentially identical except that one refers to the development of the
Lakeview/Altrudy Property and the other the redevelopment of the stater Brother Shopping center. For purposes of
simplitication in our further analysis. we will ref'er to thr t*o diflerent properties colleaivjy as ttre
developmenuredevelopment of the properties that are the subject of the governmental decisions.
To make this determination, the appraiser then appears to list three "factors determining
the impact of the development/redevelopment [of the properties that are the subject of the
governmental decisions on Councilmember Horton's real property]." Presumably, these are the
only factors looked at by the appraiser to make his determinationT that rhe
developmenUredevelopment of the properties that are the subject of the governmental decision
will have "no effect, negative or positive, on CouncilmembeiHorton's rial property." The
factors examined are: will the govemmental decisions relating to the
development/redevelopment of the properties (l) affect the dJvelopment, (2) affect the use, or
(3) result in a change to the character of the neighborhood of Couniilmember Horton's real
property.
These same factors appear in Regulation 18705.2(bXl), which provides the materiality
standard for indiredly involved real property interest and certain factori that may be used to
rebut that presumption. The regulation provides:
"(b) Indirectly involved real property interests.
"( I ) Real property, other than leaseholds. The financial effect of a governmental
decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the govemmental decision is
presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there
are specific circumstances regarding the governmental decision, iis financial effect,
and the nature of the real property in which the public official has an economic
interest, which make it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on the real property in which the public official has an interest.
Examples of specific circumstances that will be considered include, but are not
limited ra, circumstances where the decision affects:
"(A) The development potential or income producing potential of the real
property in which the official has an economic interest;
"(B) The use of the real property in which the official has an economic
interest;
"(C) The character of the neighborhood including, but not limited to,
substantial effects on: traffic, view, privacy, intensity of use, noise levels, air
emissions, or similar traits of the neighborhood." (emphasis added.)
What are listed in the regulation are examples of circumstances that are affected by a
governmental decision that could be used to rebut a presumption of no materiality for indirectty
involved property. While they may be appropriately considered to rebut a presumption of
materiality, they are certainly not the only factors to be considered. Here, hbwerer, the appraiser
treats these factors as if they are the exclusive determining factors to rebut a presumption-of
materiality of the financial effect on directly involved property.
7 At least as far as what is contained in the reports; no other analysis being provided
Because the report is limited to these factors, it does not adequately address the issue of
whether the deve_lopmenUredevelopment of the properties will have any financial effect on
Councilmember Horton's real property (Regulation 18705.2(a)(l). Th; report reaches the
conclusion that the development/redevelopment of the properties that are tire subject of the
govemmental decisions will have "no effect, negative or positive," on Councilmimber Horton,s
real property because the development/redevelolment will not cause an increase in the traffic in
the neighborhood, or increase the noise, or affect the view. While these are factors to consider in
any analysis, they are most certainly not the.only factors to consider that may impact a property,s
value, especially when the impact need only b" on" p"*y.
The appraisal provides no analysis or discussion of why the development/redevelopment
of the properties that_are the subject of the govemmental decision would not have eyen a one-
penny affect on the Horton property. The appraisal, in fact, does not even address the one-penny
materiality rule at all. That factor alone renders the appraisal inadequat" ro, furposes of
rebutting the presumption of materiality, because it does not acknowledge the appropriate test to
be applied for directly involved property.
The analysis also asserts that the improvements will not be "similar enoqgh to use as
comparable properties in determtning property value" (Emphasis added). Apparently, the
appraiser makes this-statement in support of his finding thaf the develop*entiied"velopment will
have no financial effect on the Horton property. HowJver, just because u p.op"rty,s value is
estimated by looking at sales of similar properties, it does not follow that development of
dissimilar (noncomparable) properties will 
-have 
no impacton a specific property,s value solely
because the improvements made do not consist of comparable single family homes. If so, that
would mean that a home's value would be impacted if a lesser quutity house was built on the lot
behind it, but it would not if a fast-food restaurant was put in instead.
The sales comparison approachto value is premised on the fact that adjustments are made
for differences between similar (even identical or "tnodel match',) houses. One of those
adjustments is for location. The desirabilityof a property's location is, in part, oetermined by
what surrounds it. The closer a property todesirable surroundings - the trigher its value; the
closer to undesirable surroundings - the lower its value. Yet heie the appriisal does not identify
what the surrounding! are going to be for the Lakeview/Altrudy properil(other rhan to cite a
wide range of possibilities). If one does not know what is going io ue uuiti, how can one
possibly measure its financial impact on property nearby?
According to your facts, the LakevjeilAltrudy property "is being considered for possible
development with residential uses, a new fire station, commercial uses, or some combination
thereof'" This leaves open a wide range of possibilities. Among r"ria.rtior ,.Lr i, could include
anything from low income apartments, to condominium or townhouse type units, duplexes, or
single family residences of-varying degrees of quality of construction. with respect to
commercial uses, it could theoretically include: an orto repair shop, a drug rehabilitation center,
professional office buildings, a tattoo parlor, restaurants (fast food or gourmet or anywhere in
between), high-end boutiques, discount stores, or any number of otheibusinesses. yet here, we
are being asked to accept that the vast differen.r, orong these various choices will have no
bearing, one way or the other, on the properties nearby because none of them can be seen, heard,
or felt (through increased traffic) and none can be ured o, comparable sales for measuring the
market value of the residential properties nearby.
There are other factors that can cause a property to increase or decrease in market value
besides those considered in the appraisal reporti 1oi tisieO as "examples of, in Regulation
18705'2(b)(l)). There are even more when-we are not measuring market value per se, but, rather,
a materiality threshold of only a penny. A governmental decisioi on prop"ny located within 500
feet of an official's property that would notiause a financial effect or u t.urttne penny would
have to be completely inconsequential to the property involved. perhaps the installation of a fire
hydrant two blocks over or a pathway to a park on tnl next street, when the official,s property is
not benefitted or burdened by the pathwayind is served by a different park altogether, would
qualify as situations where there is not even_a "one-pennylffect." De;lop;;nuredevelopment
projects are not these types of decisions, and decisions involving the compfnents of a development
or redevelopment $oject are generally of such a magnitude thaithey will rarely, if ever, not have a
least a one-penny financial effect on the residential p-roperties nearby fwitfrin j6iii;;J 'vr's'v I
Even the smaller decision relating to the Stater Bros. raises the same questions. Here the
facts provide that "redevelopment of the Stater Bros. building could range from a relatively
simple remodel of the building to a complete teardown and riconstructiJn.,' It seems apparent
that most people buying homes in an $800,000 neighborhood would u" *ittirrg to pay a penny
more for their purchase to have a nicer grocery store at which to shop. yet thE appraisal
essentially concludes that they would not, a conclusion that we cannot accept without some
support.
Finally, in determining that the market value of the Horton's property is $g10,000, the
appraiser made site.adjustments of $5 per square foot on the comparable properties. The most
comparable of the three properties listed is located about a half u -it" o*uy,'*a on the other
side of a major thoroughfare (Yorba Linda Boulevard). Because the size oi th" lot is larger than
th9 -Horton property, the sale price of that home was adjusted at the aforementioned rate.
Additionally, a $2,500 adjustment was made because the home has a bathtub and/or shower in
the third bathroom. A-ccording to the appraisal reports, this means that a single square foot of lot
size has a financial effect more than 500 times {$SOl.Ot), and a bathtub more"that 250,000 times
($2'500/'01), on the value of the Horton property than tire financial effect resulting from what
may be built within a few hundred feet from it. Again, this is difficult for us to accept.
For the foregoing reasons, the information provided by these appraisal reports is not
sufficient to rebut the presumption of materiality urd"r Reguiation B71s.z(a)and cannot be

Professional Appraisal Practice that fbrm the basis for the law in california governing real estate appraisals, in
support of a finding that a new development projecl consisting of "approxim ately 24residential neighborhoods, a
commercial center, and o-ver 240 acres of parks ind open rpoJ.r" located within 5fi) t'eet of several official,s
properties (at least tlne of which appears to abut the piojeci; would not have a one-penny financial effect,n the
otficial's property.

relied upon by Councilmember Horton as proof "that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
govemmental decisions will have any financial effect" on Councilrn"*b", Horton,s property.
SrBp 6: RBesoxABLy FonrssBesrn
Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time the decision is made depeids on the facts surrounding the decision. A
financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, iut it must be more
than a mere possibility. (rn re Thorner (1975) l Fppc ops. tia.;
Ultimately' jt js up to the public official to make the determination through a good faith
effort. (Hensley Advice Letter, No. A-07-l 13: MoockAdvice L.ttr, N;.-eor-r+0, O,Harra
Advice Letter, No. 4-00-174.) However, given the magnitude of the governmental decisions
involved, coupled wip the extremely low materiality thieshold preseni, it is reasonably
foreseeable that the financial effect or tne decisions *ill *"rt the one-penny materiality
threshold.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 This letter SUPERSEDES Carvalho Advice Letters Nos., A-02-293; A-02-294; and A-02-314 April 6, 2010 M. Lois Bobak Woodruff, Spradlin & Smart 555 Anton Boulevard, Suite 1200 Costa Mesa, California 92626-7670 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-10-036 Dear Ms. Bobak: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Yorba Linda City Councilmember Jan Horton regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act of the "Act")¹ and is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws, such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTIONS 1. May Councilmember Horton participate in the city council and/or redevelopment agency's consideration of the zoning and/or possible development of the Lakeview/Altrudy Property for residential uses, a fire station, and/or commercial uses? 2. May Councilmember Horton participate in the city council and/or redevelopment agency's consideration of the zoning and/or possible development of the Stater Bros site for the same or other uses? CONCLUSIONS 1. & 2. No. Because Councilmember Horton's real property is located within 500 feet of each of the properties that is the subject of the governmental decision, she is presumed to have a ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-10-036 Page No. 2 conflict of interest in participating in the governmental decisions. You have not provided sufficient facts to rebut that presumption. FACTS The Yorba Linda City Council serves as the Board of Directors of the Yorba Linda Redevelopment Agency. Councilmember Horton and her family own a home located at 4791 Cedar Avenue in the City of Yorba Linda (the "City"). The City has a redevelopment project area that encompasses most of the City's downtown area. Councilmember Horton's family home is located outside of, but immediately adjacent to, one corner of the northwest portion of the redevelopment project area. The City also has a Town Center Master Plan. The area covered by that plan overlaps much of the redevelopment project area. The City is currently developing a Specific Plan for the Town Center. That plan will contain the zoning and development standards for the properties covered by the plan. Portions of two parcels of property located within the area covered by the Specific Plan are within 500 feet of Councilmember Horton's home.
